summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that Glances reflects an arbitrary attacker-controlled Origin header in Access-Control-Allow-Origin and sets Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, enabling cross-origin data exfiltration from any malicious website.
expected_security_impact: The Glances REST API should respond to cross-origin requests with Access-Control-Allow-Origin reflecting the attacker's Origin header and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, allowing any website to read system monitoring data via the victim's browser.
actual_observed_result: Running the reproduction container started Glances v4.5.2_dev01 in web server mode, sent a request with Origin: http://evil-attacker.com, and observed Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://evil-attacker.com and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true in the response. The /api/4/all endpoint returned full system monitoring data including CPU and memory statistics.
verification_method: The verify.sh script starts Glances in web server mode, sends an HTTP request with the Origin: http://evil-attacker.com header to the /api/4/status endpoint, and checks that the response contains Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://evil-attacker.com and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. It also requests /api/4/all and confirms the response body contains system monitoring data (cpu or mem keys). The script distinguishes CORS header reflection from a missing or restrictive CORS policy by checking exact Origin reflection.
evidence_produced_by_run: Response headers include access-control-allow-origin: http://evil-attacker.com reflecting the attacker-controlled Origin header.
Response headers include access-control-allow-credentials: true, allowing credentialed cross-origin requests.
The /api/4/all endpoint returned system monitoring data (cpu, mem) cross-origin, confirming data exfiltration is possible.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable ref, and mounts that checkout into the container.
